tool.name: recetox-xMSannotator: Compute RT Modules
tool.description: cluster peaks by retention time and integrate with peak modules

configfiles.configfile:
# Load peak table (should already have module assignments from compute_peak_modules) peak_table <- load_table("$peak_table", "$peak_table.ext")
# Compute RT-based clustering peak_rt_clusters <- recetox.xmsannotator::compute_rt_modules( peak_table = peak_table, peak_width = $peak_rt_width )
# Save output save_table(peak_rt_clusters, "$output_file", "$output_file.ext")

param.name: peak_table
param.label: Peak table with module assignments
param.help: Peak table that should include: - peak: Peak identifier - rt: Retention time - module: Module assignment from Compute Peak Modules tool - Additional intensity columns as needed

tool.help:
**Compute RT Modules**
Cluster peaks based on retention time proximity and integrate with correlation-based peak modules.
Description ===========
This tool performs retention time (RT)-based clustering of peaks. Peaks derived from the same compound should elute at similar retention times. By combining RT clustering with correlation-based module assignments, we can more confidently group related peaks.
The algorithm groups peaks that fall within a specified RT window and assigns them RT cluster identifiers. These RT clusters are then integrated with the correlation-based peak modules.
Parameters ----------
**Peak RT Width** Expected chromatographic peak width in seconds. This determines the RT window for clustering: - Narrow peaks (LC-MS): 0.5-1.5 seconds - Wider peaks (GC-MS or slow LC): 2-5 seconds - Very wide peaks (comprehensive 2D LC): 5-10 seconds
Set this based on your chromatography conditions. A good rule of thumb is to use the average peak width from your chromatogram.
Inputs ------
**Peak Table with Module Assignments** A peak table that includes: - `peak`: Peak identifier - `rt`: Retention time in seconds - `module`: Correlation-based module assignment (from Compute Peak Modules) - Intensity columns for each sample
Outputs -------
**RT Cluster Table** A table containing: - `peak`: Peak identifier - `rt_cluster`: RT-based cluster assignment - `mean_intensity`: Average intensity in the RT cluster - `module`: Original correlation-based module (preserved) - Additional RT cluster statistics
Integration with Downstream Tools ---------------------------------
After computing RT modules, the typical workflow is:
1. Join RT clusters back to annotation table 2. Re-compute mass defect on the enriched peak table 3. Continue with isotope detection
The RT cluster information helps: - Isotope detection: Isotopes must be in the same RT cluster - Adduct grouping: Adducts should co-elute - Confidence scoring: Multiple ions in same RT cluster increase confidence
Usage Notes -----------
**Combining with Annotation:** To integrate RT clusters with your annotation, perform an inner join on the peak column:
``` annotation ← JOIN annotation WITH rt_clusters ON peak ```
**Quality Control:** Check the distribution of RT cluster sizes: - Many singleton clusters may indicate too narrow peak width - Very large clusters (>100 peaks) may indicate too wide peak width
References ----------
Tsugawa, H., et al. (2020). MS-DIAL 4: Accelerating lipidomics using coeluting, isotope, and in-source adduct ion annotations. Journal of Lipid Research, 61(5), 802-814. DOI: 10.1194/jlr.D11900043
